IN THE 14th CIRCUIT COURT FOR THE COUNTY OF MUSKEGON, MICHIGAN 
990 Terrace St Muskegon Michigan 49442 
Case No.: 2023-5907-PP (PPO) & 2024-001507-DC (Custody) 
Honorable Judge: Jenny McNeill 
 
Plain

Legal Basis for Reopening Custody Case 
Under MCL 722.27(1)(c), the court has the authority to modify or reopen a custody order if 
there has been a significant change in circumstances or proper cause
Plaintiff, Andrew J. Pigors, pursuant to MCR 2.505 and MCR 3.206, respectfully moves this Court to reopen the custody case and consolidate it with the PPO case (2023-5907-PP) due to overlapping facts

5. MCR 7.306 — Extraordinary Writs

Canon 1.text: A judge should participate in establishing, maintaining, and enforcing high standards of conduct, and shall personally observe those standards so that the integrity and independence of the judiciary will be preserved.

Canon 2.text: A judge should not hold membership in any organization that practices invidious discrimination.

Canon 3.text: A judge should perform the duties of the office impartially and diligently.
Canon 3.examples: ["[medium] • Service of this order within 24 hours via MiFILE plus first‑class mail with SCAO proof of service filed.", "[low] I certify that on 2025-10-07, I served this filing via MiFILE electronic service to registered service contacts and by first‑class mail to addresses of record, and will file the SCAO Proof of Service ", "[critical] Authority lock: Use current MCR PDF (May 1, 2025) as the rule spine; consult MJI Civil Benchbook for checklists and tables; keep SCAO forms as the canonical templates for summons/complaint; follow MiF", "[high] If any claim triggers federal jurisdiction or §1983, the OS flips to FRCP+WDMI LCr for pleading, e-filing, division assignment, and Rule 11 compliance. Otherwise it stays in MCR/SCAO/MiFILE lane.", "[low] For state filings: enforce MiFILE format/KB guidance and SCAO forms; for WDMI: enforce PDF standards and local rule constraints.", "[high] ⚖️ Motion Generators (MC, FOC, §1983, Strike, Show Cause, Canon Complaint)", "[critical] [CASE_LAW] 484 Mich 247: suggests due process was lacking. Michigan courts (e.g., Hunter v. Hunter, 484 Mich 247) recognize", "[low] [CASE_LAW] 451 Mich 470: | Cain v. Dept of Corrections, 451 Mich 470 | Judicial disqualification |", "[low] Ensure that you request a Friend of the Court (FOC) review of your case, particularly if Emily’s PPO or financial situation affected parenting time.", "[low] FOC Child Support Review: Request a FOC review of child support calculations to ensure that errors are identified and corrected, particularly in light of your proposed 50/50 arrangement."]

Canon 4.text: A judge may engage in activities to improve the law, the legal system, and the administration of justice.

Canon 5.text: A judge should regulate extra-judicial activities to minimize the risk of conflict with judicial duties.

constitutional_violations.description: Parenting time suspended via ex parte order without notice or hearing — 52 ex parte orders total with 44% rate, 100% favoring Emily Watson
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976) — three-factor balancing test for procedural due process; Fuentes v. Shevin, 407 U.S. 67 (1972) — prior notice and hearing required before deprivation

constitutional_violations.description: Judge McNeill sent biasing letter to HealthWest evaluator after first eval returned ALL ZEROS, resulting in changed "Rule Out" finding
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Ward v. Village of Monroeville, 409 U.S. 57 (1972) — judicial bias violates due process; Caperton v. A.T. Massey Coal Co., 556 U.S. 868 (2009)

constitutional_violations.description: Ex parte order based on recording made in violation of MCL 750.539c (felony eavesdropping) by Lori Watson — fruit of the poisonous tree
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Mapp v. Ohio, 367 U.S. 643 (1961) — exclusionary rule; Wong Sun v. United States, 371 U.S. 471 (1963) — fruit of the poisonous tree

constitutional_violations.description: Repeated denial of opportunity to present evidence and cross-examine witnesses at critical hearings affecting parental rights
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970) — right to hearing before deprivation; Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill, 470 U.S. 532 (1985)

constitutional_violations.description: Fundamental liberty interest in parenting severed for 217+ days without compelling state interest — NEGATIVE drug screen, ALL ZEROS eval, 9 police investigations with ZERO findings
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Troxel v. Granville, 530 U.S. 57 (2000) — parental rights as fundamental liberty interest; Santosky v. Kramer, 455 U.S. 745 (1982) — clear and convincing evidence standard

constitutional_violations.description: 59+ days incarceration for civil contempt without adequate purge conditions or proportionality review
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Turner v. Rogers, 564 U.S. 431 (2011) — procedural safeguards for civil contempt incarceration; Bearden v. Georgia, 461 U.S. 660 (1983)

constitutional_violations.description: 52 ex parte orders with 100% granted to mother (Emily) and 0% to father (Andrew) — statistical impossibility absent discrimination. 44% ex parte rate far exceeds Michigan average.
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Craig v. Boren, 429 U.S. 190 (1976) — intermediate scrutiny for gender; United States v. Virginia, 518 U.S. 515 (1996) — exceedingly persuasive justification required

constitutional_violations.description: Pattern of adverse rulings against pro se father while accommodating represented mother — disparate treatment based on representation status
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Bounds v. Smith, 430 U.S. 817 (1977) — meaningful access to courts; Turner v. Rogers, 564 U.S. 431 (2011)

constitutional_violations.description: Escalating sanctions and adverse orders in apparent retaliation for filing complaints (JTC, bar complaint, appellate filings) — chilling effect on right to petition
constitutional_violations.caselaw: BE&K Construction Co. v. NLRB, 536 U.S. 516 (2002) — First Amendment protects litigation activity; Borough of Duryea v. Guarnieri, 564 U.S. 379 (2011) — Petition Clause

constitutional_violations.description: 59+ days incarceration constitutes seizure of person without probable cause for criminal conduct — civil contempt used as criminal punishment
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Albright v. Oliver, 510 U.S. 266 (1994) — Fourth Amendment seizure; County of Sacramento v. Lewis, 523 U.S. 833 (1998)

constitutional_violations.description: Incarcerated for civil contempt exceeding 6 months cumulative without appointed counsel — when liberty is at stake, right to counsel attaches
constitutional_violations.caselaw: Turner v. Rogers, 564 U.S. 431 (2011) — alternative procedural safeguards; Argersinger v. Hamlin, 407 U.S. 25 (1972) — right to counsel when imprisonment imposed
